coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots originated from chitin nanofibers as a fluorescent chemoprobe for drug sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Naghdi, Tina
Atashi, Mojgan
Golmohammadi, Hamed
Saeedi, Iman
Alanezhad, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from chitin nanofibers (CNFs) as a new carbon source via microwave assisted-hydrothermal route within three minutes. The FL of as-prepared CQDs can be quenched after addition of Cu2+ ions. However, in the presence of d-Penicillamine (DPA), Cu2+ ions prefer to bind to DPA owing to their high affinity toward DPA and subsequently the FL of CQDs is effectively recovered. Therefore, here for the first time the Cu2+ ion modified CQDs (CQDs-Cu2+) was utilized as a FL chemoprobe for quantitative determination of DPA in biological and pharmaceutical samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Chitin nanofibers
Fluorescence
Drug sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recommended procedure for determination of DPA using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+
Optical and structural characterizations of the as-prepared CQDs
DPA sensing using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+
Optimization of chemical variables on DPA sensing
Analytical performance of fabricated CQDs as chemosensor

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (left) and FL spectra (right) of fabricated CQDs with variation of excitation wavelengths (290–430nm). The inset is the optical images of fabricated CQDs illuminated under white (left) and UV lamp with 365nm light (right). (B) TEM images and DLS analysis (inset) of the fabricated CQDs. (C) FTIR spectra of the CNFs (blue curve) and the fabricated CQDs (red curve). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Schematic representation for DPA sensing using the fabricated CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions (B) FL spectra of fabricated CQDs with various concentrations of Cu2+ ion in the range from 0 to 10μgmL−1 and (C) FL spectra of the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+ with various concentrations of DPA in the range from 0 to 50μgmL−1.
(A) Influence of pH and (B) Cu2+ ion concentration on determination of 1.0μgmL−1 of DPA by using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+. Each number is average of three determinations.
Calibration plots of DPA sensing by using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+ (A) in the range 0.1–50μgmL−1 (B) in the range 0.1–1μgmL−1 and (C) in the range 1–50μgmL−1 of DPA.

The influence of coexisting substances on the FL response of the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+ toward DPA (DPA: 2μgmL−1,K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl− (10,000μgmL−1), NH4 +, HCO3 −, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Protein, Sucrose, Glucose (1000μgmL−1), Uric acid (up to 200μgmL−1), Glutathione (up to 75μgmL−1), Ascorbic acid, Tyrosine (up to 50μgmL−1), Cysteine, Glycine(up to 20μgmL−1), Zn2+ (up to 5μgmL−1) and Fe3+ (up to 2μgmL−1). No interfering: The mixture of CQDs-Cu2+ in the presence of DPA without coexisting substances.
Determination of DPA in real samples.
Comparison of analytical performance of different methods for DPA detection.

row.entry: SWV a /CPE a modified with TiO2 NPs and QZ a
CV a and DPV a /electrocatalytic oxidation with KI at the surface of GCE
HPLC/derivatization with CMQT a , separation by IPRC a and UV detection
Kinetic assay/ligand substitution reaction and spectrophotometeric detection
Fluorimetric/Cu2+-induced FL quenching system of AuNCs a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Abbreviations; SWE: square wave voltammetry; CPE: carbon paste electrode; QZ: quinizarine; MIP-GCE: molecularly imprinted polypyrrole modified glassy carbon electrode; CV: cyclic voltammetry; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry; CMQT: 2-chloro-1-methylquinolinium tetrafluoroborate; IPRC: ion-pair reverse phase chromatography; CD: circular dichroism; CdS-QDs: cadmium sulfide-quantum dots; VPG-FTIR: vapor-phase generation and Fourier transform infrared; NBD-CL: 4-chloro-7-nitrobenzo-2-oxa-1,3-diazole; DCQ: 2,6-dichloroquinone-4-chlorimide; AuNCs: gold nanoclusters; Ref.: reference.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots originated from chitin nanofibers as a fluorescent chemoprobe for drug sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Young Researchers and Elite Club, Ahvaz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Protection Agency of Khuzestan, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran, 14335-186, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Lorestan University, Khoramabad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from chitin nanofibers (CNFs) as a new carbon source via microwave assisted-hydrothermal route within three minutes. The FL of as-prepared CQDs can be quenched after addition of Cu2+ ions. However, in the presence of d-Penicillamine (DPA), Cu2+ ions prefer to bind to DPA owing to their high affinity toward DPA and subsequently the FL of CQDs is effectively recovered. Therefore, here for the first time the Cu2+ ion modified CQDs (CQDs-Cu2+) was utilized as a FL chemoprobe for quantitative determination of DPA in biological and pharmaceutical samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to unique, fascinating and remarkable physicochemical characteristics of nanomaterials relative to their bulk counterpart, they have been widely exploited in various research fields, such as chemistry, physics, biology, engineering and industrial applications. However, despite the many beneficial properties of nanomaterials, their unique toxicological properties and also the toxicity of the most of methods that are utilized to produce them on human health and environment is one of the major concerns for their applications [1–5]. To tackle these challenges, recently the use of bio-based nanomaterials with biological and environmental benign composition and finding the green approaches for the synthesis of nanomaterials with minimum generation of toxic compounds has been of strong interest [4–14].

Among the bio-based nanomaterials, recently CQDs as a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials have acquired increasing attention in chemical/biosensing, bioimaging and photo/electrocatalysis applications because of their excellent physicochemical and photochemical stability, high FL, easy fabrication and functionalization, low toxicity, good aqueous solubility, low cost and abundant nature compared to conventional semiconductor QDs [13–18]. CQDs can be synthesized practically via “top-down” synthetic routes such as electrochemical carbonization, laser ablation and arc discharge by breaking down the larger carbon structures such as graphite, graphite oxide and activated carbon to CQDs, also “bottom-up” synthetic routes such as chemical oxidation, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment and microwave irradiation methods that fabricate the CQDs and polymer dots by dehydration and further carbonizating the carbon precursors such as carbohydrates, fruit juices and other biomasses in elevated temperatures [13–21]. Microwave assisted-hydrothermal synthesis is one of the most often-cited green, low-cost and fast approaches to create CQDs. During the microwave radiation, the molecules of carbon precursors can absorb sufficient thermal energy from microwave radiation, which subsequently leads to increase the temperature of sample. By temperature increasing, carbon sources can chemically change to produce CQDs by first condensing, dehydration and depolymerization of carbon precursors in solution media, and following that decomposition and carbonization of the remaining solid [23,24].
Herein, we report for the first time the synthesis of CQDs from CNFs via one-pot microwave-assisted hydrothermal route and explore the efficiency of as-prepared CQDs for DPA sensing.

DPA with the chemical name of (2S)-2-amino-3-methyl-3-sulfanylbutanoic acid is a natural amino-thiol that can readily chelate the heavy metal ions such as copper to form complexes. It has been recognized as a drug to treat various disorders including Wilson’s disease (hepatolenticular degeneration), primary biliary cirrhosis, rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, cystinuria, progressive systemic sclerosis and heavy metal poisoning [25–28]. However, DPA at high concentration levels is toxic and can cause some side effects such as myasthenia, ageusia or dysgeusia rash, pemphigus, thrombocytopenia and so on [29]. Therefore, there has been considerable interest for DPA sensing in the past years by numerous methods such as fluorometry [26–28], spectrophotometry [30–35], chemiluminescence [36], electrochemistry [35–42], capillary electrophoresis [43] and chromatography [44,45]. Most of the aforementioned analytical techniques for the determination of DPA suffer from the use of toxic, carcinogenic and expensive reagents, sophisticated and expensive equipment, matrix interferences and insufficient sensitivity. Hence, notwithstanding the current improvements in DPA sensing, development of efficient, sensitive, selective, cost-effective and environmentally friendly methods for determination of DPA in pharmaceutical and biological samples is still demanded.

CNFs, as one of the bio-based nanomaterials with a typical width less than 100nm, are produced from chitin compounds which are mainly found in the exoskeletons of many organisms such as crustaceans, insects, fungi, algae and etc. Because of fabrication the CNFs from chitin as the most abundant natural aminopolysaccharide and the second most plentiful bio-based polymer after cellulose, they possess the beneficial and inherent features of natural chitin such as non-toxicity, renewability, biodegradability, sustainability and biocompatibility. On the other hand, owing to their extended crystalline structures, high aspect ratio and other exceptionally unique characteristics of nanomaterials, CNFs exhibit the excellent physical and mechanical properties. The aforementioned fascinating features of CNFs make them one of the most desirable bionanomaterials for diverse applications [10–12,22].

During the preliminary experiments it was found that the FL intensity of as-prepared CQDs could be dramatically quenched by Cu2+ owing to binding the Cu2+ as a FL quencher to the surface of CQDs. But, in the presence of DPA, Cu2+ ions prefer to bind to DPA and subsequently are released from the surface of CQDs owing to their high affinity toward DPA. Hence, upon addition of DPA the FL of CQDs-Cu2+ is recovered (“turn-off-on” FL strategy). The recovered FL intensity of CQDs-Cu2+ was linearly proportional to the concentration of DPA and was utilized as an analytical signal for DPA sensing. The analytical characteristics of the developed method for quantitative determination of DPA were also evaluated for linearity, sensitivity, selectivity and analyzing the biological and pharmaceutical samples.

All chemicals were of analytical grade and double distilled water was used throughout the experiments. A 1000μgmL−1 copper stock solution was prepared by dissolving 268mg of CuCl2·2H2O (Merck, Germany) in exactly 100mL of water. A 2500μgmL−1 stock solution of DPA was prepared by dissolving 250mg of DPA (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) in water and diluting to 100mL in a volumetric flask. CNFs (containing 3wt% solid content 97% deionized water) were kindly provided by Nano Novin Polymer Co. (Sari, Iran).

The absorbance and FL measurements were performed by using a spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC, Japan). TEM images and DLS analysis of fabricated CQDs were performed by a transmission electron microscope (Zeiss-EM10C-80 KV, Germany) and a particle size analyzer (Scatteroscope I; Qudix, Inc., South Korea), respectively. FT-IR spectrum was recorded on a FT-IR spectrometer (Vertex-70 series, Germany). A microwave digestion system (Ethos TC, Milestone, Italy) was utilized for fabrication of CQDs.

The CQDs were synthesized from CNFs via one-pot microwave assisted-hydrothermal method, which is known as an efficient, facile and low-cost technique for large quantity synthesis of CQDs. In a typical procedure, 3g of CNFs was mixed with 20mL of solution of saturated CaCl2 in ethanol and then transferred into a Teflon-lined double-walled digestion vessel (50mL). The sealed vessel was then heated by using a microwave digestion system at a controllable temperature of 180°C (1000W) for 3min. During this process, the color of the reaction mixture was changed from colorless to light brown, implying the successful conversion of CNFs to CQDs. After microwave irradiation, the vessel was cooled down naturally to room temperature. The resulting solution was then centrifuged (4000rpm) for 15min to discard large or agglomerated particles. Finally, the fabricated CQDs solution was stored at 4°C under dark conditions before being used. It was found that the synthesized CQDs are stable for more than 2 months without any changes in their FL intensity. The absorption and FL spectra (at different excitation wavelengths) of fabricated CQDs have been presented in Fig. 1 A. The TEM image, DLS analysis and FT-IR spectra of the CNFs and fabricated CQDs have also been depicted in Fig. 1B and Fig. 1C, respectively.

The FL quantum yield (Φ) of fabricated CQDs was determined using following expression when quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (Φ=0.54) was used as the standard:
Φ =
Φ
R
I
I R
A R
A
η
2
η R
2
Where I, A and η are the measured FL intensity, absorption value at the excitation wavelength (370nm) and refractive index of solvent used, respectively, and the subscript “R” refers to the reference (quinine sulfate). In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorbance of CQDs and reference solutions (λ ex =370nm) were adjusted at values less than 0.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recommended procedure for determination of DPA using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In typical experiments for the determination of DPA, 2.5mL of as-prepared CQDs solution, 1.0mL of acetate buffer (0.05M, pH 5), 0.5mL of Cu2+ solution (50μgmL−1) and aliquots of DPA solutions with different concentrations (so that its concentration would be in the range of 0.1–50.0μgmL−1) were added to a 5mL volumetric flask and then diluted with water. After 15min, the FL spectra of the test solutions were measured (at λ ex =370nm and λ em =480nm). A blank solution was also prepared in the same way without adding any DPA solution.

For determination of DPA in pharmaceutical samples, a solution of DPA was prepared by dissolving an accurately weighed quantity of five capsules (Cupripen 250: containing 250mg of DPA, purchased from Laboratories Rubio, S.A. (Spain)) in water and diluting to 250mL in a volumetric flask. The contents of the flask were sonicated for 10min and finally filtered through a filter paper (Whatmann, 0.45μm Milli-pore). Then, 1mL of this solution was transferred into a 100-mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with water to give a final concentration of 50μgmL−1. An aliquot of this solution was subjected to the recommended procedure for determination of DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterizations of the as-prepared CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A shows the UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of the fabricated CQDs. The resulting CQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent emission phenomenon so that their FL emissions shift to longer wavelength with increasing the excitation wavelengths. They have strong blue FL with maximum emission intensity at 480nm under the excitation at 370nm. The quantum yield of fabricated CQDs was also estimated to be 5.1% according to aforementioned equation.
The TEM image and DLS histogram of fabricated CQDs given in Fig. 1B clearly reveal that they have been well dispersed with a spherical shape and the diameters in the range of 2–12nm.

The functional groups in the CNFs and fabricated CQDs were identified by FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1C). The broad absorption bands around 3400cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of OH/NH groups. The characteristic absorption peaks at approximately 1635, 1558 and 1316cm−1 are commonly assigned to amide I, II and III bands, respectively. The absorption peaks around 1450cm−1 and 2900cm−1 are due to the C-H bending and stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption bands ranging from 1075 to 1155cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric bridge oxygen and CO stretching. The good water solubility of fabricated CQDs can be due to the presence of OH/NH and amide functional groups in their structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPA sensing using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+

ce:section.ce:para: The sensing strategy for DPA sensing by using the as-prepared CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions has been schematically depicted in Fig. 2 A. The FL intensity of as-prepared CQDs was dramatically quenched by increasing the concentration of Cu2+ ions (Fig. 2B). The as-prepared CQDs have amide and OH functional groups which can form complexes with Cu2+ ions. As a result of this complex formation and subsequently due to binding the Cu2+ ion as a FL quencher to the surface of CQDs and the electron transfer from the photo-excited CQDs to Cu2+, the FL intensity of as-prepared CQDs was effectively quenched after the addition of Cu2+ ions. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 2C, the FL of the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+ is recovered upon addition of DPA as an analyte, because in the presence of DPA the Cu2+ ions prefer to bind to it due to their high affinity toward DPA and formation of soluble Cu2+-DPA complex in solution and subsequently releasing the quencher (Cu2+ ions) from the surface of CQDs which can lead to an increase in their FL intensity (“turn-off-on” FL strategy). The complexing ability and affinity interaction of DPA with Cu2+ ions has been recognized and utilized in various studies [25,36,46]. The recovered FL intensity of CQDs-Cu2+ was linearly proportional to the concentration of DPA and was utilized as an analytical signal for DPA sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of chemical variables on DPA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the highest possible analytical signal for determination of DPA by using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+, the optimum conditions were established by investigating the influence of chemical variables such as pH of the sample solution, type of the buffer and concentration of quencher (Cu2+ ion).

The pH of the sample solution is one of the important analytical parameters that can be effective on the DPA sensing by using the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+, because it can affect the ionic strength, activity and charges of Cu2+ ions, DPA and CQDs and consequently the affinity and complex formation between them. Therefore, to evaluate the influence of pH on the sensing process, the procedure of DPA sensing was examined in the pH range of 3–10. According to the results shown in Fig. 3 A, maximum analytical signal for the determination of 1.0μgmL−1 of DPA was obtained at pH 5.

The effect of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions ranging from 1.0 to 20.0μgmL−1 on the DPA sensing was also investigated due to significant role of Cu2+ ions in selective determination of DPA (Fig. 3B). As can be seen in this figure, the highest analytical signal for determination of 1.0μgmL−1 of DPA was observed when Cu2+ ions concentration was 5.0μgmL−1 in the final solution. Thus, for every measurement of DPA, Cu2+ ion concentration of 5.0μgmL−1 was regarded as optimum concentration of the quencher.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of fabricated CQDs as chemosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical characteristics of the recommended method for the DPA sensing were evaluated under the optimum conditions. The calibration curve was linear over two different ranges of 0.1–1.0μgmL−1 and 1.0–50.0μgmL−1 of DPA with linear regression equations F =0.193C +0.289 (r=0.996) and F =0.008C +0.506 (r=0.994), respectively, where F is the analytical signal and C is the concentration of DPA in μg mL−1 (Fig. 4 ). The limit of detection (S/N
= 3) for DPA was 0.09μgmL−1. The relative standard deviation for 6 replicate determinations of 2.0μgmL−1 of DPA was 2.9% that verifies the reproducibility of developed method for the DPA sensing.

The selectivity of the proposed method for the DPA sensing was assessed by performing the competitive experiments at 2.0μgmL−1 of DPA in the presence of some (bio)chemicals probably existing in human blood serum, urine and pharmaceutical samples under the optimal conditions stated above. The results of this assay given in Fig. 5 indicate the good selectivity of the developed method toward DPA, because the FL intensity of the mixture of CQDs-Cu2+ at 2.0μgmL−1 of DPA was little affected (less than ±5% from the analytical signal of DPA) by the presence of K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl− (up to 10,000μgmL−1), NH4 +, HCO3 −, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Protein, Sucrose, Glucose (up to 1000μgmL−1), Uric acid (up to 200μgmL−1), Glutathione (up to 75μgmL−1), Ascorbic acid, Tyrosine (up to 50μgmL−1), Cysteine, Glycine (up to 20μgmL−1), Zn2+ (up to 5μgmL−1) and Fe3+ (up to 2μgmL−1).

To further explore the applicability of the developed method for the DPA sensing in real samples analysis; it was utilized for the determination of DPA in human blood serum, urine and commercial pharmaceutical formulations. The sample solutions of DPA capsules were treated according to the mentioned procedure (Section "Preparation of pharmaceutical samples"). Each of real samples was separately spiked with two different concentration levels of DPA and analyzed. Three measurements were carried out at each concentration. The obtained results of this assay (Table 1 ) reveal that the proposed method can detect DPA without being affected by the matrices of the analyzed biological and pharmaceutical samples.

The analytical characteristics of the proposed method for DPA sensing have significantly improved over some of the existing reported methods. A more detailed comparison with other various reported methods for determination of DPA is provided in Table 2 .

Overall, herein we have demonstrated for the first time a simple and facile one-pot microwave assisted-hydrothermal route for preparing the water soluble photoluminescent CQDs with size distribution of 2–12nm and FL quantum yields of 5.1% from CNFs within three minutes. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by TEM, DLS and spectroscopic analysis such as UV–vis, FL and FT-IR spectroscopy. We have also shown that CQDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions can be successfully applied to the determination of DPA in human blood serum, urine and pharmaceutical samples. The sensing strategy for the determination of DPA is based on gradually recovering the quenched FL of CQDs-Cu2+, upon the addition of DPA concentration due to the formation of soluble Cu2+-DPA complex and subsequently releasing the quencher (Cu2+ ion) from the surface of CQDs. Important parameters affecting the determination of DPA with CQDs-Cu2+ were optimized to improve the sensitivity of recommended method. The fabricated CQDs have noteworthy advantages such as simple, rapid, cost effective and environmental friendly fabrication process, high stability as well as high performance for efficient, sensitive and selective FL sensing of DPA in different matrixes with satisfactory results.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Chemistry & Chemical Engineering Research Center of Iran (Tehran, Iran) and Environmental Protection Agency of Khuzestan (Ahvaz, Iran) is gratefully acknowledged. The financial support of the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council is also greatly appreciated.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of chitin nanofibers and their composites
